entries.title: navigation3

entries.title: Overview

The changes include a clearer introductory sentence, formatting updates, and the addition of an `IMPORTANT` note. These changes clarify the API's purpose and provide developers with a concise understanding of its application for collecting end-user performance profiles.

entries.title: Retrieve and analyze profiling data
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to use the standard markdown callout blocks (`> [!NOTE]`, `> [!IMPORTANT]`) for notes and key points, improving readability and consistency. This change makes the information more prominent and easier to understand.

entries.title: Profiling limitations
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to clarify how rate limiting works in the ProfilingManager. It provides more detail on the cost assigned to profiles, the time periods used for rate limiting (hourly, daily, weekly), and the errors that can occur when limits are reached. Additionally, the document now emphasizes that the system-level rate limiter is a global limit shared by all apps.

entries.title: Android vitals
entries.summary: This document provides minor updates to improve clarity and formatting. Changes include clarifying that Android vitals uses user-perceived metrics, updating links, and minor text enhancements.

entries.title: Excessive Mobile Network Usage in Background
entries.summary: The documentation on background network usage was updated to improve clarity and formatting. Changes include rewording sentences, adding links, and updating the warning about Battery Historian. These changes aim to provide more accurate and accessible information for developers to optimize their app's network usage and battery performance.

entries.title: Excessive Wi-Fi Scanning in the Background
entries.summary: The documentation on background Wi-Fi scanning has been updated for clarity and consistency.  It clarifies when an app is considered to be running in the background and provides more specific links and formatting improvements.  These changes enhance readability and provide more accurate information for developers.

entries.title: Crashes
entries.summary: The documentation for Android app crashes has been updated to include minor formatting changes and improve clarity.  The note regarding Wear OS apps has been changed to use the correct formatting.

entries.title: Excessive battery usage
entries.summary: The documentation for excessive battery usage in Wear OS watch faces has been updated with minor formatting changes, emphasis on key information using callout boxes, and clarifications. These updates aim to provide clearer guidance on optimizing watch face apps for battery efficiency and reducing visibility penalties in Google Play.

entries.title: Excessive partial wake locks
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to clarify how Android Vitals identifies excessive partial wake lock usage, including the threshold (2 or more hours in a 24-hour period) and the exemptions applied. Additional details on the Android Vitals dashboard are provided, along with the impact on app visibility on Google Play.

entries.title: App startup time
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to provide a more comprehensive explanation of app startup, including why fast startup is important for user experience and retention. It also includes an expanded explanation of the different app startup states (cold, warm, and hot) and how to identify and measure startup time using Perfetto. The introductory text was rewritten, and the document includes additional details about startup time metrics (TTID, TTFD) and provides more context on the processes involved in app launch.

entries.title: Low memory killers
entries.summary: This document has been updated to include better formatting, improve clarity, and adds more information for the Unreal Engine's Memory Insights. These changes enhance the readability and comprehensiveness of the documentation.

entries.title: Permission Denials
entries.summary: This document provides guidance on how to handle Android app permissions, focusing on user privacy and engagement. It explains how Android Vitals helps gauge user behavior by tracking permission denials and offers best practices to minimize them, such as requesting only necessary permissions and explaining them in context.

entries.title: Slow rendering
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated with minor formatting changes, added a "Note" block, and improved clarity in several sections. Specifically, the introduction was reworked, and the Systrace section was enhanced with updated links and more information about alerts and custom trace events.

entries.title: Slow Sessions (games only)
entries.summary: This document introduces the "Slow Sessions" metric in Android Vitals, a new metric in the Google Play console used to measure frame-rate performance of games. It explains what constitutes a slow session, how to measure frame rates using `dumpsys surfaceflinger timestats`, and provides solutions using Android Frame Pacing (Swappy), Vulkan, and ADPF.

entries.title: Stuck partial wake locks
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to improve readability and clarity. Changes include better formatting, more descriptive language, and improved organization. This makes the documentation easier to understand and follow, which helps developers to diagnose and fix stuck wake lock issues in their Android applications more effectively.

entries.title: Excessive wakeups
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated with minor formatting changes, including improved readability and clarity through the use of line breaks and enhanced formatting of code snippets and API references. It also contains slight improvements in wording for better comprehension.

entries.title: Create App Links for Instant Apps
entries.summary: The documentation for Android Instant App Links has been updated. The changes clarify the requirements for app links in instant apps, emphasizing the need for both HTTP and HTTPS support in intent filters. It also reiterates the rules regarding domain association and provides more detailed reminders for creating app links, including assetlinks.json requirements and best practices for host attributes.

entries.title: Limit loading in on-device Android containers
entries.summary: The documentation clarifies the definition of "On-device Android containers" and adds a sentence explaining how to prevent these containers from loading or proxying a Play Store app. It also improves formatting.

entries.title: Get a result from an activity
entries.summary: This document provides more formatting changes, adds note blocks, and clarifies the use of `registerForActivityResult()` and `ActivityResultRegistry` with lifecycle management. These changes improve readability and provide better guidance for developers using the Activity Result APIs.

entries.title: Sending the user to another app
entries.summary: The documentation for sending intents was updated to include a video, fix broken links, and improve clarity. It also updated text for better formatting and more accurate information.

entries.title: Android for Cars overview
entries.summary: The documentation for Android Auto has been updated to reflect the use of the > [!NOTE] admonition for notes instead of the markdown bold format. Also the video application description was updated to remove a space between the asterisks.

entries.title: Add support for Android Auto to your templated app
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to clarify the location of the `automotive_app_desc.xml` file within the project directory and to slightly reword a sentence for clarity. This is important for developers to correctly set up their Android Auto applications.

entries.title: Add support for Android Automotive OS to your templated app
entries.summary: This document provides guidance on building Android Automotive OS apps using the Car App Library. It covers the necessary steps, including creating an automotive module, declaring support in the manifest, updating Gradle dependencies, and declaring CarAppService and CarAppActivity.

entries.title: Build an internet of things app
entries.summary: The documentation for IOT apps has been updated to improve readability and clarity. The changes include reformatting text for better flow and adding links to relevant resources, ensuring developers have the necessary information to build IOT car applications. No new functionality was introduced.

entries.title: Use the Android for Cars App Library
entries.summary: The documentation for the Car App Library was updated to include an IMPORTANT block, replacing the previous markdown formatting with a callout block. This change emphasizes Google's strong stance on driver distraction and the requirements for apps to be listed on Google Play for Android Auto and Android Automotive OS, clarifying the importance of adhering to these guidelines for app developers.

entries.title: Car Hardware APIs
entries.summary: The documentation for the Car Hardware API was updated to improve clarity and formatting. Specifically, example code snippets were added for checking vehicle heading, and a caution regarding CarSensors data on Android Automotive OS was enhanced with a more prominent warning style and formatting. These changes aim to assist developers in utilizing car hardware features, particularly on Android Automotive OS, by providing clearer usage examples and highlighting important platform-specific limitations.

entries.title: Record from the car microphone
entries.summary: The documentation updates include minor wording adjustments and formatting changes in the "car-microphone.md" file. There are no substantial modifications impacting functionality or introducing new features. Therefore, the changes are considered trivial.

entries.title: Create your CarAppService and Session
entries.summary: This document describes the implementation of `CarAppService` and its `onCreateSession` method. The changes include minor wording adjustments and formatting updates to improve readability, but do not introduce new functionality.

entries.title: Connection API
entries.summary: The documentation updates the warning regarding CarConnection API and Android 14 compatibility. It clarifies that apps targeting Android 14 and higher must use Car App Library version 1.3.0-beta01 or later to avoid exceptions related to receiver export behavior. Apps targeting Android 13 or lower are not affected by this change.

entries.title: Constraints API
entries.summary: The documentation for the constraints-api.md file was updated to use the Android Markdown syntax for an important alert. This change highlights that the host sets the content limits, and these limits cannot be modified by client apps. The link to the integers.xml resource file remains the same.

entries.title: Draw maps
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to clarify how to handle theme changes. It also added a new admonition. This matters because it provides developers with better guidelines to render their app based on the current system theme.

entries.title: Add inline CarIcons for rows
entries.summary: The documentation for inline car icons has been updated to use a more modern callout block for the note regarding API level compatibility. This improves readability and consistency with the current documentation style.

entries.title: Install the Car App library
entries.summary: The documentation updated the emphasis on the use of alpha and beta releases of the Jetpack library for car apps. It now uses a callout box to highlight the importance of exercising caution when using newly introduced or experimental APIs in those releases, as they may lead to compatibility issues.

entries.title: Request permissions
entries.summary: The documentation for requesting permissions in Android Auto apps was updated to use the markdown warning block. This change replaces the inline warning with a markdown warning block, improving readability and highlighting the dependency on `androidx.car.app:1.7.0-alpha01` or later for correct permission dialog behavior on Android 14+ devices. This is important to ensure that apps correctly prompt the user for permissions when run on compatible Android Auto devices.

entries.title: Implement screen navigation
entries.summary: The documentation for screen navigation in Android Automotive has been updated. The changes include a minor wording adjustment to clarify the behavior of back actions in message templates and how new screens are pushed.

entries.title: Set up your project

entries.title: Create your start screen

entries.title: Template restrictions

entries.title: Add text string variants
entries.summary: The documentation provides examples and instructions on how to use text string variants with CarText in Android Automotive apps. The changes include minor formatting adjustments and link updates.

entries.title: Build a templated media app
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to reflect the use of Markdown note and caution blocks instead of bolded text for emphasis. The manifest and API level requirements, along with the code example, remained unchanged.

entries.title: Build a navigation app
entries.summary: The document was updated with minor changes to the manifest file examples for Android Automotive OS navigation apps. These changes primarily involve fixing potential typos and formatting inconsistencies within the XML code, ensuring proper declaration of services, intent filters, and permissions. These updates are essential for maintaining the accuracy and usability of the code examples for developers building navigation apps.

entries.title: Build a point of interest app
entries.summary: This document provides guidance on developing Point of Interest (POI) apps for Android Auto. It covers how to declare category support, access map templates, refresh content, and integrate with Google Assistant using App Actions.

entries.summary: The document was updated to use the `androidx.car.app.category.POI` category instead of the deprecated `androidx.car.app.category.PARKING` and `androidx.car.app.category.CHARGING` categories. Also, the documentation was updated to clarify how to refresh the PlaceListMapTemplate content, and some minor formatting and wording changes.

entries.title: Build a point of interest app
entries.summary: The changes ensure that developers are using the correct and current categories for their POI apps, aligning with the latest Car App Library versions. The refresh content update helps developers implement content refresh functionality in their apps.

entries.title: Build a weather app
entries.summary: The documentation for weather apps has been updated to clarify the purpose of weather applications in cars, emphasizing their ability to provide weather information for current locations or along a route, and offering navigation capabilities.  The document also updates formatting, link structures and emphasis.

entries.title: Car ready mobile apps
entries.summary: This document outlines the Car ready mobile apps program, which allows eligible mobile apps in the video, games, and browser categories to be distributed to cars with Android Automotive OS. It details eligibility requirements, testing procedures, and how to participate and opt-out of the program. The document has been updated to clarify the launch date, the availability of apps, and the impact of the program on app distribution.

entries.title: Build communication apps for Android Auto
entries.summary: The document has been updated to use a more descriptive introductory paragraph and a more modern callout block for the tip. This change provides a better overview of communication features on Android Auto, improving readability and user experience.

entries.title: Build calling experiences for Android Auto
entries.summary: The documentation for calling apps has been updated to use the `> [!IMPORTANT]` syntax for important notes. This change improves the visual emphasis of the critical information for developers, making it more noticeable. Additionally, it clarifies the requirement to include both calling and messaging categories in the same intent filter if an app supports both features.

entries.title: Extend messaging notifications to Android Auto
entries.summary: The documentation for Android Auto messaging notifications has been updated. These updates improve readability and clarify important information for developers integrating messaging features with Android Auto. The documentation also clarifies the use of `PendingIntent.FLAG_MUTABLE`.

entries.title: Build templated messaging experiences for Android Auto
entries.summary: The documentation for templated messaging has been updated to use the 'Note' and 'Tip' syntax using the markdown syntax. This change improves the visual clarity and formatting of the document for developers, making important information stand out more effectively.

entries.title: Distribute to cars
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of how to distribute Android apps for Android Auto and Android Automotive OS (AAOS). It outlines guidelines and requirements for different app categories to help developers successfully launch their apps on these platforms.

entries.title: Media apps for cars overview
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to reflect formatting changes, improved code examples, and more helpful content regarding the development of media apps for Android Auto and AAOS. Formatting and some wording were updated in several sections, including the section about creating audio media apps. The documentation has also added or updated some CAUTION and NOTE blocks.

entries.title: Add support for Android Auto to your media app
entries.summary: The documentation for Android Auto media support has been updated. The changes include a minor formatting adjustment to emphasize the key information regarding existing media browser service implementation by using a callout. This change aims to improve clarity and highlight the prerequisites for integrating media apps with Android Auto.

entries.title: Add Android Automotive OS support to your media app
entries.summary: The documentation for Android Automotive OS media apps was updated. Changes include rewording, improved formatting with admonitions, and clarifying the steps for project setup, particularly manifest configuration and Gradle dependencies.

entries.title: Configure manifest files
entries.summary: This document describes how to configure your app's manifest files to integrate with Android Auto and Android Automotive OS (AAOS), focusing on declaring the media browser service and specifying app icons, labels, and accent colors.  Changes include minor formatting adjustments, clarifying descriptions, and updating code snippets for better readability.

entries.title: Media browser service overview
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of creating a media browser service for Android Auto and Android Automotive OS (AAOS). The primary change involves clarifying the classes to extend (MediaBrowserServiceCompat or MediaLibraryService) and how Android Auto/AAOS utilizes the service for content browsing and media playback control. This matters because it provides essential context for developers building media applications for car platforms, ensuring compatibility and proper functionality within the Android Auto/AAOS environment.

entries.title: Display browsable search results
entries.summary: This document describes how to enhance the search experience in Android Auto and Android Automotive OS by enabling searches without voice and displaying search results. The changes include clarifying the purpose of the page, refining the descriptions of how to support search, and updating formatting.

entries.title: Build your content hierarchy
entries.summary: This document introduces updates to the content hierarchy implementation for Android Auto and Android Automotive OS (AAOS) media browser services. The changes include clarification and better formatting of code snippets, and updated package validation guidance including allowing Google Assistant access to the MediaBrowserService. It also highlights the importance of root menu structure, tab limits, and the use of root hints to optimize content display.

entries.title: Apply content styles
entries.summary: This document introduces changes related to content styles in Android Automotive OS (AAOS) media applications. It clarifies how to apply content styles for browsable and playable items, including setting defaults using BrowserRoot extras and overriding styles for specific items or their descendants. The updates also provide clearer guidance on grouping media items using title hints, emphasizing the importance of contiguous blocks for correct grouping and explaining how the order of items impacts the grouping logic.

entries.title: Implement custom browse actions
entries.summary: This document describes how to implement custom browse actions in an Android MediaBrowserServiceCompat for Android Auto. It explains how to define actions, attach them to MediaItems, handle user interactions, and update action states. The document has been updated with minor improvements to clarify the implementation steps, and formatting.

entries.title: Media browser service lifecycle
entries.summary: This document describes the interaction between Android Automotive OS (AAOS) and Android Auto with a media browser service. The changes improve formatting, clarity, and readability by adding emphasis and fixing minor issues.

entries.title: Display media artwork
entries.summary: The documentation updates the formatting of the warning and note sections by replacing the HTML-style formatting with the markdown-style formatting. This change improves the visual presentation and readability of the documentation.

entries.title: Implement distraction safeguards
entries.summary: This document explains how to implement distraction safeguards in Android Auto media apps. It focuses on preventing automatic audio playback, such as alarms, and managing how advertisements are displayed to minimize driver distraction. The document provides instructions on using the `CarConnection` API and setting the `METADATA_KEY_IS_ADVERTISEMENT` key.

entries.title: Enable playback control
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to use the `> [!NOTE]` syntax for callout boxes instead of the previous `| **Note:**` format for note sections. This change improves the visual consistency and clarity of the documentation.

entries.title: Handle errors
entries.summary: The documentation for handling errors in Android Auto and Android Automotive has been updated to clarify how error messages are displayed and how to address errors. It provides additional guidance on providing user-facing and localized error messages, including a specific recommendation to instruct users to interact with their phone if necessary. The changes improve clarity and provide more specific guidance on implementing error handling in media applications for cars.

entries.title: Support voice actions
entries.summary: This document provides guidance on integrating voice actions into Android Auto and Android Automotive OS (AAOS) media apps. The changes mainly focus on improving readability and clarity, including formatting code, adding a note, and refining the explanation of how voice actions are handled by apps.

entries.title: Build parked apps for cars
entries.summary: The documentation for parked apps has been updated to include information about support for different display sizes, and more emphasis on app distribution. The changes provide more guidance on how to optimize apps for cars and how to distribute them through Google Play.

entries.title: Add support for Android Auto to your parked app
entries.summary: This document introduces changes related to Android Auto app development. The updates include enhanced formatting, added warnings and tips using the `[!CAUTION]`, `[!IMPORTANT]`, and `[!TIP]` callouts, and clarified guidance on supporting different screen sizes and detecting Android Auto usage.

entries.title: Add support for Android Automotive OS to your parked app
entries.summary: This document provides guidance on developing Android applications for Android Automotive OS, covering testing, manifest configuration, and driver distraction requirements. The changes update the formatting of warnings and tips, add more emphasis on driver distraction, and clarify the use of CarUxRestrictionsManager for handling UX restrictions.

entries.title: Build browsers for Android Automotive OS
entries.summary: This document provides information on developing browser applications for Android Automotive OS. The changes improve formatting using markdown features like admonitions and adjust phrasing for clarity. The update emphasizes security requirements for handling sensitive user data, particularly in shared vehicle environments, and provides guidance on implementing device credential authentication.

entries.title: Build games for cars
entries.summary: The documentation for games on Android Auto and Android Automotive OS has been updated. The changes include rephrasing the introductory paragraph, updating the formatting for important and caution notes using markdown callouts, and refining the text for clarity. These changes provide more direct and clear information for developers integrating games for parked car experiences.

entries.title: Build video apps for Android Automotive OS
entries.summary: The documentation updates the video.md file. It converts the existing emphasis on design guidelines, and important notes into the NOTE and IMPORTANT blocks. It also updates the codelab link, and the caution sections into a caution block.

entries.title: Android Auto overview
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to use the 'Note' and 'Caution' blocks for emphasizing important information. It also refactored the formatting to use the standard markdown for Android documentation.

entries.title: Android Automotive OS overview
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to replace the use of the markdown bold syntax for notes and tips with the more structured admonition syntax using `> [!NOTE]` and `> [!TIP]`. This change improves the document's structure and potentially its rendering on different platforms.

entries.title: Coarse location on Android Automotive OS
entries.summary: The documentation for coarse location in Android Automotive OS has been updated. The primary change involves replacing the original note with a callout block, which indicates that automotive apps shouldn't rely on NLP or sensor fusion performed by the fused location provider (FLP).

entries.title: Android Automotive OS compatibility mode
entries.summary: This document describes Android Automotive OS compatibility mode, a software feature in certain cars to improve the user experience of mobile apps on Android Automotive OS. The changes include clarifications and reformatting of existing content, including more details regarding Back navigation, Safe area rendering, Density scaling, and Activity lifecycle. Additionally, the content was updated to include a caution about how this behavior applies to all apps running in compatibility mode, not just apps running in compatibility mode, and to update details around testing apps in compatibility mode.

entries.title: Google Play on Android Automotive OS
entries.summary: This document provides information about Google Play's functionality within Android Automotive OS. It details which Google Play services, libraries, and APIs are supported, as well as specific considerations for in-app purchases and licensing on automotive platforms. The changes mainly involve clarifying the capabilities and limitations of Google Play features in cars with Google built-in, and providing updated links and formatting.

entries.title: Google Play services for cars with Google built-in
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to clarify that when developing apps for Android Automotive OS cars with Google built-in, certain Google Play services might be restricted to ensure a safe and consistent experience. It also emphasizes that developers can use the same client libraries for Google Play services as they do for mobile apps. The updated content now directly states that the provided table illustrates which Google APIs for Android are available on Android Automotive OS cars with Google built-in, and the table itself remains unchanged.

entries.title: Notifications on Android Automotive OS
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of how notifications function on Android Automotive OS. It details the differences in notification behavior compared to other Android devices, focusing on simplified user interactions, UX restrictions based on drive state, and specific requirements for messaging notifications to ensure driver safety and minimize distractions.

entries.title: Releases
entries.summary: This document details changes related to Android versions 12-16 that affect Android Auto and Android Automotive OS. It includes updates regarding window insets, foreground service types, runtime-registered broadcast receivers, activity launching, the locale of CarContext, UI mode, component exporting, and pending intent mutability to ensure app compatibility and correct behavior on different Android versions.

entries.title: Test Android apps for cars
entries.summary: This document introduces updates to the Android Auto and Android Automotive OS testing documentation. The changes include formatting adjustments, clarification of existing content, and minor wording improvements to enhance readability and provide better guidance to developers on testing their car applications.

entries.title: Test using Android Automotive OS on Pixel Tablet
entries.summary: This document has been updated to improve readability, clarity, and formatting.  Specifically, it uses callout boxes for notes, cautions, and important information.  It also updates links, and reformats the table in the "System images" section to improve readability.

entries.title: Test using the Desktop Head Unit
entries.summary: This document provides instructions for using the Desktop Head Unit (DHU) to test Android Auto apps. The update clarifies the steps for connecting to the DHU using Accessory Mode and ADB Tunneling, which is important for developers testing Android Auto apps.

entries.title: Test using the configurable emulator
entries.summary: This document describes how to configure the Android Automotive emulator to mimic various hardware configurations seen in cars. entries.title: Test using the distant display emulator
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to improve readability and formatting. Changes include line breaks, clearer descriptions, and the use of a TIP block.

The changes include improvements in formatting and wording for clarity, as well as minor adjustments to the instructions for setting up the demo application and the inclusion of the required code snippets for handling intents.

entries.title: What's new

Key changes include updates to existing codelabs, the addition of new sections on browser apps, media artwork, virtual displays, and custom metadata indicators, along with the availability of the AppSearch module and the addition of Android 16 information.

entries.title: Designing for foldables
entries.summary: This document describes features added to ConstraintLayout 2.1 to support foldable devices, including SharedValues, ReactiveGuide, and enhanced MotionLayout support for animations. These features enable developers to manage UI elements and animations based on the state of the foldable device, such as the fold position or device orientation.

entries.title: MotionLayout reference
entries.summary: The document introduces MotionLayout and motion scenes. It clarifies that a motion scene file is an XML file that defines a motion sequence, referenced by the `app:layoutDescription` attribute in the layout file's `<MotionLayout>` node. This overview helps developers understand the core concepts and setup for using MotionLayout.

The documentation for `<Constraint>` element in MotionLayout has been updated. A video link has been added, and the documentation now explicitly states that the `<Constraint>` element supports standard ConstraintLayout attributes, directing users to the ConstraintLayout developer guide for details. This update provides direct access to video tutorials and clarifies the element's behavior.

The documentation for `ConstraintSet` has been updated with a link to a YouTube video and some minor formatting changes for clarity. Specifically, a video link was added and some lines were reformatted for better readability.

The documentation for `<KeyFrameSet>` has been updated to include a link to a video explaining how to use it. It also refines existing explanations for clarity and readability, clarifying how `<KeyFrameSet>` modifies the behavior of MotionLayout animations by using KeyPosition and KeyAttribute to specify intermediate states.

The documentation for `<KeyPosition>` has been updated to include a link to a video tutorial, improve the explanation of the element's purpose, and refine the descriptions of attributes. These changes enhance clarity and provide users with a better understanding of how to use `<KeyPosition>` to customize motion paths in MotionLayout.
The documentation for `motionscene.md` has been updated with minor formatting changes, clarifying language, and improved syntax. A video link has also been added at the beginning of the file.
The documentation for `<OnClick>` in MotionLayout has been updated. A video link has been added, and minor text refinements improve clarity, such as breaking up long sentences for better readability.

The documentation for `OnSwipe` has been updated with minor formatting improvements, and a link to a video tutorial has been added. These changes enhance readability and provide additional learning resources for developers.

The documentation for `<Transition>` in MotionLayout has been updated. A video link has been added, and minor adjustments were made to the text, including formatting and wording improvements for better clarity. The changes clarify how to specify start and end states within the transition definition.

entries.title: Data and file storage overview

entries.title: Access app-specific files
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to reflect UI changes. It replaces the old "Note" and "Caution" callouts with new "NOTE" and "CAUTION" UI elements to improve readability. There were also minor wording adjustments for clarity.

entries.title: Save data in a local database using Room
entries.summary: The documentation for Room has been updated to improve readability and clarity. Changes include better formatting, improved explanations, and code examples. Some sections were also updated to improve clarity, such as the note about choosing between `ksp` and `annotationProcessor` in the build.gradle file. Additional formatting changes were done to improve the appearance, and some minor wording changes were made.

entries.title: Accessing data using Room DAOs
entries.summary: This document introduces Data Access Objects (DAOs) in Room, explaining their role in interacting with a database. It covers the anatomy of a DAO, including convenience methods for basic operations (insert, update, delete) and query methods for more complex interactions, with examples in Kotlin and Java.

entries.title: Write asynchronous DAO queries
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to modernize the formatting with the use of markdown callouts instead of the deprecated bolded "Note:" for important information and to update the layout for the table. These changes improve readability and maintain consistency with current documentation practices.

entries.title: Create views into a database
entries.summary: The documentation introduces and explains the usage of SQLite database views within the Room persistence library, starting from version 2.1.0. It details how to create views using the `@DatabaseView` annotation, including an example, and how to associate them with the database through the `@Database` annotation's `views` property. These views allow encapsulating queries and are treated similarly to data objects within DAOs, enabling complex data retrieval operations.

entries.title: Define data using Room entities
entries.summary: The documentation for defining Room entities has been updated to clarify concepts, improve clarity, and provide more detailed explanations of features like FTS and AutoValue integration. The changes include reformatting, enhanced notes, and more specific examples.

entries.title: Migrate your Room database
entries.summary: This document describes how to migrate Room database versions. It explains automatic and manual migration strategies, the use of `AutoMigrationSpec` for complex schema changes in automated migrations, and the process of testing migrations using the `room-testing` Maven artifact, including schema export and configuration options for different Room versions and build variants.

entries.title: Prepopulate your Room database
entries.summary: This document introduces prepopulating a Room database, explaining how to initialize a Room database with data from a prepackaged database file. It covers prepopulation from assets and the file system, along with how it affects database migrations.

entries.title: Referencing complex data using Room
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to clarify the use of type converters, including how to handle their initialization and scoping. It also reinforces the reason why Room doesn't allow object references, explaining the performance implications of lazy loading on the UI thread and suggesting the use of POJOs and joins for referencing multiple entities.

entries.title: Prepare your app for behavior changes in Wear OS 4
entries.summary: Wear OS 4 introduces new permission requirements for apps. Notably, apps now require notification runtime permission by default and background body sensors permission. Apps should also account for approximate location permission. This affects how apps request and handle user permissions for notifications, background sensor access, and location data.

entries.title: Prepare your app for behavior changes in Wear OS 4
entries.summary: Significant changes to app components and navigation are detailed, including stricter intent filter matching, modified root launcher activity behavior, stricter Android App Links verification, and the removal of the SYSTEM_ALERT_WINDOW UI. These changes impact how apps handle intents, lifecycle management, deep linking, and UI display.

entries.title: Prepare your app for behavior changes in Wear OS 4

entries.title: Test how your app handles behavior changes

It highlights changes affecting all apps and those specifically for apps targeting Android 14, including privacy dashboard support, watch face format requirements, backgrounding of always-on apps, foreground service type declarations for exercise-recording apps, device unlock duration, handling of draggable content, restrictions to implicit intents, and non-dismissible notifications.

entries.title: Explore features

These features improve the user experience and offer developers more tools for creating engaging and informative Wear OS apps.

entries.title: What's new for enterprise in Android 10
entries.summary: This document outlines changes in Android 10 related to enterprise features. Key updates include improved provisioning tools for work profiles, device-ID attestation for work profiles, and enhancements for work profile management such as device-wide blocking of app installations from unknown sources.

entries.title: What's new in Android 7.0
entries.summary: This document outlines the new features and API changes in Android 7.0 (API level 25) related to Android enterprise functionality. These updates enhance security, device management, and user experience within a work profile environment.

entries.title: What's new in Android 7.0
entries.summary: Key changes include support for QR code provisioning, a work profile security challenge, the ability to disable app access, work mode toggling, always-on VPN configuration, improved contact integration, remote reboot capabilities, a location off switch, customized provisioning options, multiple Wi-Fi CA certificate support, customized lock screen messages, and work profile connection services.

entries.title: What's new in Android 7.0
entries.summary: These changes improve enterprise device management by providing administrators with more control over devices, enhancing security with features like work profile challenges and always-on VPN, and improving the user experience with features like contact integration and the work mode toggle. This enables more secure and manageable corporate environments.

entries.title: What's new in Android 8.0
entries.summary: Android 8.0 introduces significant enhancements for enterprise Android deployments. Key changes include the ability to create work profiles on fully managed devices, enabling better separation of work and personal data. Additionally, user affiliation features allow for more device-wide control and functionality based on organizational identity. Several security improvements are also included, such as enhanced password reset and network logging capabilities.

entries.title: What’s in Android 9 for enterprise apps
entries.summary: This document outlines the enterprise-focused changes in Android 9. Key updates include UI enhancements for work profiles, APIs for cross-profile app switching, and expanded lock task mode capabilities. These changes aim to improve user experience and control in managed device scenarios.

entries.title: behavior-changes-all.md
entries.summary: Android 17 introduces a limit on the number of keys an app can create in the Android Keystore. Non-system apps targeting Android 17 or higher are limited to 50,000 keys, while all other apps, including system apps, are limited to 200,000 keys. If an app exceeds this limit, key creation will fail with a KeyStoreException, and the getNumericErrorCode() method will return different error codes depending on the target API level. This change prevents apps from creating an excessive number of keys, which could impact the performance of other apps on the device.

entries.title: Contact picker
entries.summary: This document introduces the Android Contact Picker, a privacy-focused and standardized interface for apps to request contact information from users. The Contact Picker, available on Android 17 (Jelly Bean) and later, provides a secure alternative to requesting the broad `READ_CONTACTS` permission. Instead of accessing the entire address book, apps specify the required data fields (e.g., phone numbers, email addresses), and the user selects which contacts to share. The document explains how to integrate the Contact Picker using `Intent.ACTION_PICK_CONTACTS` and `Intent.EXTRA_REQUESTED_DATA_FIELDS` to specify the desired contact data (using MIME types from `ContactsContract.CommonDataKinds`). It provides Kotlin and Java code examples for launching the picker in both single and multi-selection modes (using `Intent.EXTRA_ALLOW_MULTIPLE` and `Intent.EXTRA_SELECTION_LIMIT`). The document also details how to handle results, including how to query the returned `Session URI` using a `ContentResolver` to access the selected contact data, demonstrating how to process the retrieved data using a `Cursor`. It includes important considerations such as backward compatibility with the legacy `ACTION_PICK` intent, testing on older SDKs (using `EXTRA_USE_SYSTEM_CONTACTS_PICKER`), and best practices: Requesting only necessary data fields; persisting data immediately (as the session URI grants temporary access); and avoiding reliance on account data due to privacy protections.  This documentation aims to guide developers in using the Contact Picker to enhance user privacy and offer a consistent and user-friendly contact sharing experience.

It highlights the ability to display content across both screens, enabling features like using the rear camera for high-resolution selfies, facilitating group video calls, and providing real-time language interpretation. The document includes a visual example, showcasing expanded and compact views on a dual-screen device. It then links to Android Adaptive UI samples, encouraging exploration of optimized designs for large screen devices, and provides a link to a Figma kit for UI/UX templates. Finally, it addresses the implementation of a supporting pane for mobile views, offering resources from Android developers and Material Design websites to provide more information on keeping primary views focused and uncluttered.

The optimization included refining the app layout for tablets and introducing a tabletop layout for hands-free video calling on foldables. The document includes navigation links to other relevant sections of the Android Developers documentation, such as Design & Plan, UI Design, and the Gallery. A sample image showcases the optimized interface. The core message is that Google Duo's design has been adapted to provide a better user experience on devices with larger screens, enhancing user engagement and improving app ratings, especially during times when video calls were essential.

entries.title: Social media multi-tasking
entries.summary: This document introduces multi-tasking features on Android, specifically focusing on how to design UI for large screen devices, such as tablets. It highlights two key multi-tasking features: Picture-in-Picture (PiP) and Multi-window.  PiP allows videos or video calls to be displayed in a small, floating window while other content is visible, optimizing the use of large screen real estate. The document also describes Multi-window functionality, which enables side-by-side app usage, effectively providing a dual-app experience within a single screen. The document is supplemented with visual examples illustrating both features. In addition to outlining multi-tasking concepts, the document also directs readers to resources for Android Adaptive UI samples, including a Figma kit, and provides links and visual references to help developers implement supporting panes and list-detail layouts, which are critical components for optimized UI on large screen devices, using material design guidelines.

entries.title: Social media editing tools
entries.summary: This document, titled 'tools.md,' is a new addition to the Android documentation and focuses on providing content creation tools within a supporting pane layout, particularly for large screen devices. The primary purpose is to enable content creators to easily adjust settings, access color palettes, and apply effects while instantly seeing the changes. The document includes two key sections: First, the document illustrates the concept with an image of what this may look like, followed by example images of how the UI renders. Second, it highlights the 'Android AdaptiveUI Samples,' offering UI/UX templates for various app categories and providing a link to a Figma kit for exploration. It also discusses the implementation of a 'supporting pane,' which is a canonical layout element for Android that can contain supplementary content or controls to keep the primary view focused. Links to further documentation on canonical layouts and supporting panes are included. Essentially, the document introduces a design pattern for Android applications, especially those targeting large screens, to enhance the content creation workflow by providing readily accessible tools and controls alongside the main content display area, ensuring an uncluttered user experience.

entries.title: FlexBox
entries.summary: This document introduces `FlexBox` in Android Compose, a layout container for arranging items in a single direction (horizontal or vertical). It's designed to resize, wrap, align, and distribute space among items to optimize the use of available space, making it suitable for layouts with varying item sizes and responsiveness to screen size changes. The document emphasizes that `FlexBox` is heavily influenced by the CSS Flexible Box Layout specification, sharing similar concepts like main and cross axes, main and cross sizes, and terminology such as 'grow', 'shrink', and 'basis'. The document explains when to use `FlexBox` (displaying a small number of items within a larger layout, or when wrapping items) and when not to (for large numbers of items, use lazy lists/grids or for overall screen layouts, use `Grid`). It details how to apply properties to the `FlexBox` container (e.g., direction, wrap, justifyContent, alignItems) and individual items (e.g., basis, grow, shrink, alignSelf). Finally, the document outlines the layout algorithm of `FlexBox`, detailing the steps involved in calculating child sizes, sorting items, building lines, and aligning/resizing items on both the main and cross axes. It includes links to other guides for container and item behaviors, and for getting started with `FlexBox`.

entries.title: Set container behavior
entries.summary: This document introduces the configuration options for the `FlexBox` container in Android Compose, a layout element used for arranging items in a flexible manner. It explains how to customize `FlexBox` behavior using a `FlexBoxConfig` block. The primary focus is on the properties within `FlexBoxConfig` that control the layout: `direction`, `alignItems`, `alignContent`, `justifyContent`, `wrap`, `rowGap`, `columnGap`, and `gap`.  The `direction` property sets the main axis (either `Row` or `Column`, with reverse variants), determining the primary direction items are laid out.  `justifyContent` is used to align and distribute items along the main axis, offering options like `Start`, `Center`, `End`, `SpaceBetween`, `SpaceAround`, and `SpaceEvenly`.  `alignItems` controls alignment along the cross axis within a single line, providing options like `Start`, `End`, `Center`, `Stretch`, and `Baseline`.  `alignContent` manages the alignment and spacing of multiple lines (when wrapping is enabled), offering similar options to `justifyContent`.  The `wrap` property enables multi-line layouts; options include `NoWrap`, `Wrap`, and `WrapReverse`.  Finally, the document covers adding gaps between items using `rowGap`, `columnGap`, or the combined `gap` function. The documentation uses visual aids to illustrate the effects of different configuration options, including images showcasing the impact of each property on the item arrangement within the `FlexBox` container. This is a comprehensive guide to utilizing `FlexBox` effectively within Compose layouts.

entries.title: Get started with FlexBox
entries.summary: This document, titled "get-started.md," provides a beginner's guide to implementing basic `FlexBox` layouts in Android Compose. It begins by outlining the necessary steps for setting up the project, which includes adding the `androidx.compose.foundation.layout` library to the project's `lib.versions.toml` and then adding the library dependency to the app's `build.gradle.kts` file. The document then presents two examples to illustrate how `FlexBox` can be used to create layouts. The first example demonstrates a simple `FlexBox` layout with two `Text` elements stacked vertically and center-aligned. The second example showcases a more complex layout, where `FlexBox` wraps five items onto two rows, allowing them to grow unequally to fill the available space with a specified gap between items. Images are included to visualize the resulting layouts. The document concludes by directing readers to further documentation for in-depth information on `FlexBox` behavior, specifically referencing resources on "Set container behavior" and "Set item behavior."

entries.title: Set item behavior
entries.summary:
This document introduces how to control the behavior of individual items within a `FlexBox` layout in Android Compose. It details the use of the `Modifier.flex` modifier to manipulate item size, alignment, and order. The core concepts revolve around three main aspects:

1.  **Item Size:** This section covers how to manage the size of items using `basis`, `grow`, and `shrink` properties.  `basis` defines the initial or preferred size.  Different value types such as `Auto`, fixed `dp`, and percentage are explained, including behavior and example code.  `grow` determines how much an item expands to fill extra space, with examples of how different grow values affect the layout.  `shrink` addresses how items contract when space is insufficient, also detailing its behavior and examples.
2.  **Item Alignment:** This explains how to use `alignSelf` to override the `alignItems` property of the parent `FlexBox` container for specific items. The document describes the possible values for `alignSelf` (including `Auto`) and provides example code, along with a visual representation of the effect of alignment.
3.  **Item Order:** This part describes how to change the visual order of items within the `FlexBox` layout using the `order` property.  It explains that items are laid out in the order they are declared by default and how `order` can be used to reorder them without changing the code's declaration order. Example code shows how items with different order values are displayed, illustrating the impact of `order` on the visual layout.

The document provides code snippets, diagrams, and descriptions to clarify the usage and effect of each property, providing a comprehensive guide to controlling item behavior within a `FlexBox` layout.

entries.title: Bubbles
entries.summary: This document introduces the Bubbles feature, a new windowing mode in Android that provides a floating UI experience, drawing parallels to the messaging bubbles API. Users can create app bubbles on phones, foldables, and tablets by long-pressing an app icon in the launcher. The document highlights the visual representation of this feature with an example of bubbling a calendar app, demonstrated through a GIF. On larger screens, a bubble bar is incorporated into the taskbar, allowing users to organize, switch between, and manage bubbles, including anchoring them to the screen. The document emphasizes that this Bubbles feature is accessible to all apps that adhere to existing best practices for multi-window support and encourages developers to follow the guidelines to ensure proper functionality of their apps as bubbles.

entries.title: Overview  |  Gallery  |  Android Developers
entries.summary: This document, titled "Gallery", is a new addition to the Android Developers documentation and serves as a curated showcase of app designs optimized for large screens like tablets and foldables. It emphasizes the importance of adapting app UIs for various screen sizes to enhance user experience. The document is structured to inspire developers with examples from different app categories. It highlights the benefits of optimizing apps for larger screens, such as showing more content, enabling multitasking, and providing more immersive experiences. The core content focuses on how to make apps look great on different screen sizes and provides examples from multiple app domains including social media, media, productivity, shopping, reading, creativity, and games. Each section provides a brief description of how to optimize the user experience within these categories. The document also links to resources for UI design and canonical layouts, encouraging developers to create immersive and user-friendly experiences for large-screen devices.

entries.title: Creativity  |  Gallery  |  Android Developers
entries.summary: This document, titled "Creativity," is a new addition to the Android Developers documentation, specifically within the "Large Screens" and "Gallery" sections. It serves as a guide for developers on how to leverage the larger screen real estate of tablets and other large-screen devices to enhance the creative experience for users. The document focuses on features that capitalize on the potential of a tablet as a creative canvas, particularly emphasizing stylus input. It highlights key stylus features such as low latency, hover capabilities, palm rejection, and tilt/pressure sensitivity, linking to relevant Android developer resources for further exploration. The document also provides guidance on optimizing the user interface for creativity-focused apps, including utilizing more workspace for tools like palettes and settings panels. It suggests context menus, customization of the workspace (movable and resizable elements), and differentiated experiences unique to large screens. Examples of these differentiated experiences include hover-to-display, multi-window functionality, hover-to-preview, and drag-and-drop support. The document concludes with a customer story from the Concepts app, demonstrating the positive impact of large-screen optimization on user engagement, and cross-references to other related content such as Games and Social media & comm.

entries.title: Games  |  Gallery  |  Android Developers
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of how to optimize Android games for large screens, such as tablets, foldables, and devices with external peripherals. It highlights key features and considerations for creating engaging gaming experiences on these platforms. The document emphasizes the growing popularity of large screens for mobile gaming and encourages developers to leverage the unique capabilities of these devices. The core topics covered include:  1.  **Continuity**: Ensuring uninterrupted gameplay across different orientations, postures, and window sizes by supporting configuration changes gracefully. The document links to resources on handling configuration changes and cross-device play.  2.  **Game Controller Integration**: Encouraging the integration of external game controllers to enhance the gaming experience, with links to relevant SDK documentation and support information.  3.  **Mouse, Trackpad, and Keyboard Support**: Providing guidelines for incorporating mouse, trackpad, and keyboard input for fine-tuned gameplay, particularly on ChromeOS and Google Play Games on PC, with links to input support documentation.  4.  **Differentiated Experiences**: Showcasing features unique to large screens, like multi-window mode for social gaming experiences, and foldables, with guidance on optimizing in-game views and utilizing multi-modal displays.  5.  **Supported Game Engines**: Listing and linking to information on popular game engines like Unity, Unreal Engine, and Cocos, that are optimizing for large screen and multiplatform experiences.  6.  **Why Build**: Providing rationale for developing for large screens, citing the increasing market share of foldable phones (projected to reach 41M shipments by 2026) and the investment by OEMs like Samsung, Microsoft, and Lenovo in large-screen devices.  The document concludes with links to other galleries on social media and media app optimization for large screens.

entries.title: Productivity  |  Gallery  |  Android Developers
entries.summary:
This document introduces a new page within the Android Developers documentation, specifically focusing on "Productivity" within the "Gallery" section for large screens. It serves as a guide for developers to design Android applications that enhance user productivity on devices with larger displays, such as tablets and foldables. The document's primary purpose is to showcase design patterns and best practices for creating productive and user-friendly experiences on large screens, highlighting features and capabilities not easily achievable on smaller screen devices.

The content is organized into several key sections:

1.  **Introduction:** Sets the context, emphasizing the importance of adapting apps for large screens to enhance productivity and provides links to related documentation.
2.  **Core Concepts and Layouts:** This section describes layout patterns tailored for productivity, including:
    *   **File browsing and filtering:** Displaying thumbnails for easy file navigation.
    *   **Items and overviews:** Showing detailed list information in conjunction with a list.
    *   **Tools and settings (Supporting Pane):** Providing easy access to more tools and settings without obstructing the main content.
    *   **Reviews and comments (Supporting Pane):** Incorporating reviews and comments directly within the document view.
    Each layout pattern includes links to relevant Material Design guidelines and implementation guides.
3.  **Differentiated Experiences:** This section covers features that leverage large-screen capabilities:
    *   **Drag and drop:** Enables intuitive content sharing between different views or apps.
    *   **Tabletop:** Designing layouts suitable for activities like presentations and video conferences.
    *   **Multi-window:** Facilitating multitasking with apps working side-by-side.
    *   **Keyboard, mouse, and trackpad:** Enhancing productivity through input device support.
4.  **Customer Stories:** Presents real-world examples of how large screen features have been implemented to improve user experience, including Chrome and WPS Office, with links to relevant articles.
5.  **Build better with Android:** Provides links to related galleries about shopping and reading.

The "why it matters" is that it helps developers provide enhanced user experiences on large-screen devices, enabling efficient multitasking, intuitive navigation, and overall improved productivity. This, in turn, can lead to increased user engagement and satisfaction, and positions applications for success in a market increasingly focused on devices like tablets and foldables. This document provides practical guidance and promotes best practices for designing and developing Android apps that take full advantage of large-screen capabilities.

entries.title: Reading  |  Gallery  |  Android Developers
entries.summary: This document, titled "Reading" within the "Gallery" section of Android Developers documentation, provides guidance and showcases design considerations for creating optimal reading experiences on large-screen Android devices, particularly tablets and foldables. It emphasizes the advantages large screens offer, such as improved readability and immersive experiences, and suggests ways to leverage these advantages. The document is structured to highlight key features and design patterns for reading applications. The core concepts include "Full-screen readability" via immersive mode to reduce distractions, "Booklists and buys" using multi-column grids and side navigation, and "What's inside" with synopses. It also covers "Complementary content" in supporting panes, "Markup tools," and "News feeds." Furthermore, the document explores "Differentiated experiences" unique to large screens. It highlights multi-window support for multitasking, the ability to mimic a physical book's layout with "Book posture" on foldables, and "Drag and drop" functionality for content manipulation. The document includes links to relevant Android developer documentation for implementation. The document also includes a customer story from Readera LLC emphasizing the positive impact of large-screen adaptation on user reading time. Finally, it links to other areas like "Creativity" and "Games" within the Large Screens Gallery, indicating broader design considerations beyond just reading.

entries.title: Shopping  |  Gallery  |  Android Developers
entries.summary: This document introduces a new page within the Android Developers documentation, specifically focusing on the "Shopping" use case within the "Gallery" section for large screens. It aims to guide developers on designing shopping applications that effectively utilize the increased screen real estate available on larger devices, such as tablets and foldables. The document's primary purpose is to showcase how to enhance the shopping experience on large screens. It details how adaptive apps can present product information, specifications, and related details efficiently. The document outlines core concepts such as "Product specs" (using list-detail layouts), "Catalogs and promotions" (using feed layouts), and "Supporting the sale" (using supporting pane layouts). Each section provides design considerations and links to relevant Material Design guidelines and implementation resources. Furthermore, the document highlights differentiated experiences like "Multi-window" and "Drag and drop," which are not readily available on smaller screens. Finally, the document includes a customer story about eBay, emphasizing how large-screen optimizations led to a higher Google Play rating.  The document concludes with recommendations and links to content about 'Reading' and 'Creativity' user cases.
